title: Formatting Compiler Feedback
concept: Supplying the agent with exact line numbers, call stacks, and error definitions rather than vague failures.

exercises.prompt: What is the purpose of running code in a sandbox?

options.text: To capture any compiler or runtime errors.
To check if the code is perfect without mistakes.
To print the code in a beautiful format.

exercises.explain: Running code in a sandbox helps us find errors so we can fix them.

cards.title: Formatting Compiler Feedback
cards.body: When your agent gets feedback from the compiler, it's important to provide clear details, like line numbers and error types.

cards.title: What is Compiler Feedback?
cards.body: Compiler feedback tells us what went wrong in our code. It includes line numbers and error definitions to help us find and fix issues.

cards.title: Asking for Directions
cards.body: Imagine asking someone for directions to a store. If they just say, 'It's not close,' that's unhelpful. But if they say, 'At Main Street, turn left at the third crosswalk,' that's very useful!

cards.title: Example of Good Feedback
cards.body: Instead of saying 'Error found', a good feedback might say 'Error on line 10: Unexpected token'. This helps us locate and understand the problem.

cards.title: Myth: More Feedback is Better
cards.body: It's not enough to just say 'There’s an error.' Clear and specific feedback is much more useful than vague statements.

cards.title: Feedback Comparison
cards.body: Good feedback is specific and clear, helping us fix errors quickly.

exercises.prompt: What is essential for effective compiler feedback?

options.text: Including call stacks and error types.
Using fewer words to describe the errors.
Only mentioning where the code runs.
Avoiding technical language to be simple.

exercises.explain: Including call stacks and error types gives detailed information that helps fix the code errors.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Vague feedback like 'Something didn't work' is helpful for debugging.
exercises.explain: Vague feedback is not helpful; clear feedback with specifics is what we need to debug effectively.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank: The best compiler feedback includes exact ____ and error definitions.
exercises.sentence: The best compiler feedback includes exact line numbers and error definitions.
exercises.explain: Exact line numbers help pinpoint where the issue is in the code.

exercises.prompt: Which option is an example of useful compiler feedback?

options.text: Check line 8 for an unknown identifier.
It seems like something is wrong.

exercises.explain: Saying 'Check line 8 for an unknown identifier' clearly directs the user to fix the specific issue.

exercises.prompt: Why is it important to provide specifics like line numbers when giving compiler feedback?

options.text: It helps locate the error quickly while debugging.
It makes the feedback sound smarter.
It doesn't really matter; any feedback is enough.
It reduces the amount of coding we need to do.

exercises.explain: Specifics like line numbers and error types help in quickly locating and understanding the error, which improves our debugging process.
